name: Strategic Coalition Framing
consequences: Prioritizing civil-society and academic partnerships strengthens legitimacy but risks diluting technical focus. A coalition centered on privacy advocates may clash with public-sector stakeholders seeking operational continuity.
options: Form an interdisciplinary coalition emphasizing resilience and contingency planning
Leverage EU digital identity working groups to frame platform neutrality as a compliance imperative
Partner with consumer advocacy groups to highlight end-user dependency risks
review: Focusing on civil-society alliances creates credibility but neglects the procurement decision-making hierarchies within Danish public institutions.

name: Policy Narrative Reframing
consequences: Shifting from privacy-centric to resilience-focused messaging increases institutional buy-in but may understate long-term security implications of platform dependencies.
options: Position platform neutrality as a critical infrastructure resilience requirement
Anchor proposals in EU eIDAS2 compliance mandates for interoperability
Highlight supplier concentration risks in public-sector continuity-of-service policies
review: Emphasizing resilience risks overlooks the technical complexity of redefining trust models without platform-specific signals.

name: Technical Demonstrator Design
consequences: Building open-standard demonstrators validates feasibility but diverts focus from institutional adoption challenges. Browser-based solutions may face skepticism from security-critical stakeholders.
options: Develop FIDO2/WebAuthn-based authentication for GrapheneOS as a privacy-first alternative
Create a browser extension that bypasses OS-level trust signals for critical services
Implement hardware-token workflows compatible with existing eIDAS frameworks
review: Prioritizing open standards assumes institutional willingness to adopt non-proprietary solutions, ignoring legacy system integration barriers.

name: Procurement Language Influence
consequences: Influencing AltID requirements could create precedent but risks being sidelined by existing vendor relationships. Explicit fallback mandates may face legal interpretation challenges.
options: Require bid evaluation criteria that penalize exclusive reliance on platform integrity APIs
Mandate certification of fallback authentication paths under NSIS level 'Substantial'
Include contractual clauses for periodic third-party trust model audits
review: Focusing on procurement language ignores the technical expertise gap in public-sector contracting bodies.

name: Stakeholder Engagement Protocols
consequences: Regular briefings with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen build trust but may entrench dependency on a single authority. Co-design workshops risk diluting the project's technical focus.
options: Establish quarterly technical review panels with public-sector identity providers
Create a transparent reporting mechanism for demonstrator progress and limitations
Conduct scenario-planning exercises with contingency planners and crisis management teams
review: Emphasizing transparency may not address the power asymmetries in negotiations with platform vendors.

name: Interoperability Protocol Standardization
consequences: Adopting open authentication standards like FIDO2/WebAuthn reduces platform-specific dependencies but risks diluting security guarantees if implemented without rigorous validation. This could delay adoption by public-sector stakeholders wary of unproven alternatives.
options: Mandate FIDO2-based authentication for all public-sector identity workflows by 2028
Develop a hybrid authentication model combining platform signals with decentralized identifiers
Leverage EU eIDAS2 framework to codify interoperability requirements for digital identity systems
review: Standardizing protocols creates technical clarity but ignores the political calculus of platform vendors. None of the options address how to compensate or negotiate with Apple/Google to avoid retaliatory policy shifts.

name: Public-Private Certification Partnerships
consequences: Collaborating with private entities to create alternative certification pathways could accelerate adoption but introduces conflicts of interest if commercial actors prioritize profit over public-sector needs. This risks undermining trust in the fallback system.
options: Establish a joint certification body with industry leaders to validate platform-neutral authentication methods
Require third-party audits for all identity verification components used in public services
Form an industry consortium to co-develop open-source authentication infrastructure
review: Partnering with private entities creates alignment incentives but neglects the risk of vendor capture. The options fail to account for how to maintain neutrality when private stakeholders hold critical technical expertise.

name: Regulatory Sandbox Expansion
consequences: Creating a controlled environment for testing alternative authentication models enables innovation but may face regulatory pushback from agencies prioritizing stability over experimentation. This could delay critical proof-of-concept work.
options: Launch a pilot program for browser-based authentication in non-critical public services
Implement phased rollouts of hardware-token fallback mechanisms in high-risk sectors
Design cross-sector collaboration frameworks to share sandbox results with regulatory bodies
review: Sandboxing reduces implementation risk but assumes regulatory flexibility that may not exist. The options overlook how to transition successful pilots into formal procurement requirements without legislative intervention.

name: Cross-Platform Authentication Aggregation
consequences: Integrating multiple authentication methods increases system resilience but complicates user experience and maintenance. This could lead to fragmentation if not carefully managed during implementation.
options: Implement federated identity systems that unify platform-specific credentials under a single interface
Deploy decentralized authentication tokens compatible with both mobile and desktop environments
Create multi-factor authentication overlays that bypass platform-specific APIs
review: Aggregation enhances redundancy but creates new points of failure. The options do not address how to handle interoperability gaps between legacy systems and emerging standards.

name: Geopolitical Risk Insurance Framework
consequences: Quantifying geopolitical risks associated with platform dependencies strengthens procurement arguments but may be dismissed as speculative without concrete evidence of supply-chain vulnerabilities. This could weaken advocacy efforts.
options: Develop a risk-assessment model incorporating geopolitical stability indices for technology suppliers
Invest in local open-source authentication infrastructure to diversify technical capabilities
Create insurance mechanisms to offset losses from platform policy changes
review: Risk quantification provides analytical rigor but assumes linear threat scenarios. The options miss how to address non-quantifiable risks like sudden regulatory shifts or diplomatic conflicts.

name: Stakeholder Contingency Co-Design
consequences: Involving end-users and service providers in contingency planning builds ownership but may slow decision-making if conflicting priorities emerge. This could delay critical infrastructure updates.
options: Conduct co-design workshops with public-sector IT teams to map fallback scenarios
Create user-led working groups to test alternative authentication workflows
Implement iterative feedback loops between developers and service providers
review: Co-design fosters buy-in but risks overcomplicating requirements. The options fail to address how to resolve technical trade-offs when stakeholder interests diverge.

name: EU-Level Standards Advocacy
consequences: Aligning with European Digital Identity Wallet frameworks strengthens regional influence but may require compromising on national priorities to meet EU-wide standards. This could dilute Denmark's specific sovereignty goals.
options: Propose platform-neutral authentication requirements in EU technical working groups
Lobby for mandatory fallback mechanisms in European identity wallet specifications
Publish comparative analyses of Danish and EU identity frameworks to identify alignment opportunities
review: EU advocacy expands reach but creates dependency on supranational processes. The options ignore how to maintain national control when EU standards diverge from Danish needs.

name: Alternative Authentication Ecosystem Development
consequences: Building non-platform-specific authentication methods reduces reliance on Apple/Google but risks technical fragmentation and institutional resistance. A successful ecosystem could create a benchmark for interoperability, though adoption may depend on regulatory mandates rather than market forces.
options: Develop open-source authentication protocols compatible with multiple OS environments
Partner with local tech firms to co-design platform-neutral identity solutions
Establish a decentralized identity network using blockchain-based verification
review: The core tension is balancing innovation with institutional resistance; the options miss the risk of fragmented standards undermining scalability.

name: Procurement Policy Reconfiguration
consequences: Revising procurement requirements to mandate platform neutrality could force systemic changes but may face budgetary and political pushback. Success depends on aligning with existing Digitaliseringsstyrelsen priorities while avoiding overreach into operational details.
options: Mandate open standards in all public-sector identity contracts
Create a certification body to audit platform neutrality in procurement bids
Tie funding allocations to compliance with platform-neutral authentication requirements
review: The tension is between regulatory ambition and fiscal constraints; the options don't address potential cost overruns in compliance.

name: Cross-Sector Authentication Alliances
consequences: Forming coalitions across public, private, and civil society sectors could amplify influence but risks misaligned priorities. Strong alliances might pressure AltID stakeholders to adopt neutral standards, though maintaining cohesion requires continuous engagement.
options: Form industry consortia to jointly advocate for platform-neutral authentication
Engage civil society organizations to highlight democratic accountability concerns
Establish joint working groups with public-sector stakeholders to co-design fallback mechanisms
review: The tension is between collaboration and competition; the options overlook the risk of coalition fragmentation.

name: National Data Resilience Framework
consequences: Implementing localized data control measures enhances sovereignty but requires significant investment. A robust framework could serve as a fallback during platform disruptions, though technical complexity may delay deployment.
options: Establish regional data centers for critical identity verification processes
Implement end-to-end encrypted data protocols for public-sector authentication
Conduct regular public audits of data-handling practices across identity providers
review: The tension is between security and cost; the options don't account for maintenance challenges in decentralized systems.

name: EU Standards Alignment Initiative
consequences: Advocating for EU-wide interoperability standards could create momentum for platform neutrality but may clash with national interests. Successful alignment might pressure AltID to adopt broader European requirements, though regulatory friction remains likely.
options: Lobby EU institutions to include platform-neutral authentication in Digital Identity Wallet guidelines
Participate in eIDAS2 working groups to shape interoperability requirements
Harmonize Danish standards with broader European resilience frameworks
review: The tension is between EU integration and national sovereignty; the options ignore potential regulatory conflicts.

name: Public-Private Fallback Infrastructure
consequences: Co-developing fallback infrastructure with private entities could ensure continuity during platform failures but may face commercial resistance. A shared model might balance innovation with public accountability, though funding and governance remain contentious.
options: Create a public-private partnership to maintain backup authentication servers
Incentivize private-sector investment in platform-neutral authentication tools
Establish a shared infrastructure fund for emergency identity verification
review: The tension is between public accountability and private interests; the options underestimate potential profit-driven resistance.

name: Legacy System Interoperability Push
consequences: Forcing legacy systems to adopt interoperable standards could accelerate modernization but may disrupt existing workflows. Success depends on balancing technical upgrades with operational continuity, particularly in public administration.
options: Require legacy identity systems to support open authentication interfaces
Develop middleware solutions to bridge platform-specific and neutral authentication protocols
Implement phased migration plans for public-sector systems to interoperable standards
review: The tension is between innovation and operational stability; the options don't address potential downtime during transitions.
